annotations.label: Algebraic geometry

anchor.snippet: Algebraic geometry is a branch of mathematics which uses abstract algebraic techniques

annotations.note: A field-of-study description, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Algebraic varieties

anchor.snippet: The fundamental objects of study in algebraic geometry are algebraic varieties

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated classical 'algebraic variety' object; schemes (and PrimeSpectrum/zeroLocus) generalize them.

annotations.label: Point on an algebraic curve

anchor.snippet: A point of the plane lies on an algebraic curve if its coordinates satisfy a given polynomial equation

annotations.note: Membership in MvPolynomial.zeroLocus (aeval x p = 0) captures a point satisfying polynomial equations.

annotations.label: Mainstream algebraic geometry

anchor.snippet: The mainstream of algebraic geometry is devoted to the study of the complex points of the algebraic varieties

annotations.note: A descriptive remark about the discipline, not a formal statement.

annotations.label: Diophantine / arithmetic geometry

anchor.snippet: Diophantine geometry and, more generally, arithmetic geometry is the study of algebraic varieties over fields that are not algebraically closed

annotations.note: A field-of-study description, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Computational algebraic geometry

anchor.snippet: Computational algebraic geometry is an area that has emerged at the intersection of algebraic geometry and computer algebra

annotations.note: A field-of-study description, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Fermat's Last Theorem via schemes

anchor.snippet: Wiles' proof of the longstanding conjecture called Fermat's Last Theorem is an example of the power of this approach

annotations.note: The FermatLastTheorem statement is defined and exponents 3 and 4 are proven, but the full theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sphere as a vanishing set

anchor.snippet: the two-dimensional sphere of radius 1 in three-dimensional Euclidean space R 3 could be defined as the set of all points

annotations.note: Mathlib's Metric.sphere is metric, not the polynomial vanishing set of this worked example.

annotations.label: Slanted circle in R^3

anchor.snippet: which satisfy the two polynomial equations

annotations.note: This specific worked example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Affine space

anchor.snippet: We consider the affine space of dimension n over k , denoted A n ( k )

annotations.note: Mathlib's AffineSpace is the AddTorsor notion; the AG affine space A^n underlying MvPolynomial.zeroLocus (σ → K) is not a named object.

annotations.label: Polynomial (regular) function

anchor.snippet: is said to be polynomial (or regular ) if it can be written as a polynomial

annotations.note: Polynomial functions are realized via MvPolynomial evaluation (aeval), though 'regular function' is not a named predicate.

annotations.label: Ring of regular functions on A^n

anchor.snippet: the set of the regular functions on A n is a ring, which is denoted k [ A n ]

annotations.note: k[A^n] is exactly MvPolynomial σ k, which carries a CommRing instance in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vanishing set V(S)

anchor.snippet: is the set V ( S ) of all points in A n where every polynomial in S vanishes

annotations.note: MvPolynomial.zeroLocus (with zeroLocus_span) is exactly the common vanishing set of a set of polynomials.

annotations.label: Algebraic set

anchor.snippet: for some S , is called an algebraic set

annotations.note: Algebraic sets are the images of MvPolynomial.zeroLocus, but Mathlib has no standalone 'algebraic set' predicate for affine n-space.

annotations.label: Ideal I(U) of a subset

anchor.snippet: define I ( U ) to be the set of all polynomials whose vanishing set contains U

annotations.note: MvPolynomial.vanishingIdeal is exactly the ideal of polynomials vanishing on a subset.

annotations.label: Zariski topology

anchor.snippet: the Zariski topology , a topology on A n whose closed sets are the algebraic sets

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the Zariski topology on the prime spectrum with closed sets the zeroLocus sets, generalizing the topology on A^n.

annotations.label: U = V(I(U)) iff algebraic set

anchor.snippet: Then U = V ( I ( U )) if and only if U is an algebraic set

annotations.note: Mathlib proves V(I(U)) equals the closure of U on the prime spectrum, giving U = V(I(U)) iff U is closed (algebraic).

annotations.label: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz

anchor.snippet: it says that I ( V ( S )) is the radical of the ideal generated by S

annotations.note: This is exactly Hilbert's Nullstellensatz: vanishingIdeal (zeroLocus I) = I.radical over an algebraically closed field.

annotations.label: Hilbert's basis theorem

anchor.snippet: Hilbert's basis theorem implies that ideals in k [ A n ] are always finitely generated

annotations.note: Polynomial.isNoetherianRing is the Hilbert basis theorem and MvPolynomial.isNoetherianRing gives that ideals of k[A^n] are finitely generated.

annotations.label: Irreducible algebraic set

anchor.snippet: An algebraic set is called irreducible if it cannot be written as the union of two smaller algebraic sets

annotations.note: IsIrreducible (not a union of two proper closed sets) specializes to this notion under the Zariski topology.

annotations.label: Unique decomposition into irreducible components

anchor.snippet: Any algebraic set is a finite union of irreducible algebraic sets and this decomposition is unique

annotations.note: In Noetherian spaces every closed set is a finite union of irreducibles, with finite_irreducibleComponents giving the canonical (unique) components.

annotations.label: Variety

anchor.snippet: An irreducible algebraic set is also called a variety

annotations.note: The notion 'irreducible algebraic set' is expressible via IsIrreducible but Mathlib has no named affine 'variety' object.

annotations.label: Variety iff prime ideal

anchor.snippet: an algebraic set is a variety if and only if it may be defined as the vanishing set of a prime ideal of the polynomial ring

annotations.note: Mathlib proves a closed subset is irreducible iff its vanishing ideal is prime, the spectrum analogue of variety ⇔ prime ideal.

annotations.label: Regular function on an algebraic set

anchor.snippet: A regular function on an algebraic set V contained in A n is the restriction to V of a regular function on A n

annotations.note: Regular functions appear scheme-theoretically as structure-sheaf sections; the classical restriction-to-V definition is not named.

annotations.label: Tietze extension theorem

anchor.snippet: the Tietze extension theorem guarantees that a continuous function on a closed subset always extends to the ambient topological space

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the Tietze extension theorem for closed sets into TietzeExtension spaces.

annotations.label: Coordinate ring k[V]

anchor.snippet: This ring is called the coordinate ring of V

annotations.note: The coordinate ring is realizable as MvPolynomial σ k ⧸ I(V) but is not defined as a named object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: k[V] = k[A^n]/I(V)

anchor.snippet: Thus k [ V ] may be identified with k [ A n ]/ I ( V )

annotations.note: The quotient MvPolynomial σ k ⧸ vanishingIdeal exists but no lemma identifies it as the coordinate ring of V.

annotations.label: Regular map to affine space

anchor.snippet: We define a regular map f from V to A m by letting f = ( f 1 , ..., f m )

annotations.note: Regular maps are formalized scheme-theoretically as scheme morphisms, not as classical tuples of polynomials on a variety.

annotations.label: Regular map to a variety

anchor.snippet: we say that f is a regular map from V to V ′ if the range of f is contained in V ′

annotations.note: Morphisms of schemes generalize regular maps between varieties; the classical formulation is not present.

annotations.label: Morphisms and category of algebraic sets

anchor.snippet: The regular maps are also called morphisms , as they make the collection of all affine algebraic sets into a category

annotations.note: Mathlib has the category of (affine) schemes, generalizing the category of affine algebraic sets.

annotations.label: Equivalence of categories

anchor.snippet: This defines an equivalence of categories between the category of algebraic sets and the opposite category of the finitely generated reduced k -algebras

annotations.note: Mathlib proves AffineScheme ≌ CommRingᵒᵖ, the scheme-level antiequivalence, but not the classical algebraic-sets ⇔ reduced f.g. k-algebras version.

annotations.label: Function field k(V)

anchor.snippet: has thus a field of fractions which is denoted k ( V ) and called the field of the rational functions on V

annotations.note: Scheme.functionField defines the function field of an integral scheme, a field (fraction field of the coordinate ring in the affine case).

annotations.label: Rational map

anchor.snippet: one may define a rational map from a variety V to a variety V '

annotations.note: Scheme.RationalMap formalizes rational maps as equivalence classes of morphisms defined on dense opens.

annotations.label: Birational equivalence

anchor.snippet: Two affine varieties are birationally equivalent if there are two rational functions between them which are inverse one to the other

annotations.note: Rational maps are formalized but no dedicated birational-equivalence relation/isomorphism was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rational variety

anchor.snippet: An affine variety is a rational variety if it is birationally equivalent to an affine space

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of rational variety.

annotations.label: Circle is a rational curve

anchor.snippet: is a rational curve, as it has the parametric equation

annotations.note: The rational parametrization of the circle as a worked example is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Resolution of singularities (Hironaka)

anchor.snippet: It was solved in the affirmative in characteristic 0 by Heisuke Hironaka in 1964

annotations.note: Resolution of singularities is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Projective space P^n

anchor.snippet: the projective space P n of dimension n is usually defined as the set of the lines passing through a point, considered as the origin, in the affine space of dimension n + 1

annotations.note: Projectivization ℙ K V is the set of lines through the origin in a vector space, with P^n = ℙ k (k^{n+1}).

annotations.label: Homogeneous coordinates

anchor.snippet: This defines the homogeneous coordinates of a point of P n as a sequence of n + 1 elements of the base field k

annotations.note: Projectivization.mk represents a point by a nonzero vector defined up to scalar, i.e. homogeneous coordinates, but they are not separately named.

annotations.label: Vanishing on a line iff homogeneous

anchor.snippet: A polynomial in n + 1 variables vanishes at all points of a line passing through the origin if and only if it is homogeneous

annotations.note: No lemma characterizing homogeneity via vanishing on lines through the origin was found.

annotations.label: Projective algebraic set

anchor.snippet: This allows us to define a projective algebraic set in P n as the set V ( f 1 , ..., f k )

annotations.note: ProjectiveSpectrum.zeroLocus gives the vanishing set of homogeneous polynomials in the Proj setting.

annotations.label: Projective variety

anchor.snippet: The projective varieties are the projective algebraic sets whose defining ideal is prime

annotations.note: ProjectiveSpectrum points are relevant homogeneous prime ideals, but a named 'projective variety' object is not defined.

annotations.label: Decomposition of projective algebraic sets

anchor.snippet: Every projective algebraic set may be uniquely decomposed into a finite union of projective varieties

annotations.note: Finite/unique irreducible decomposition holds in Noetherian spaces, applicable to ProjectiveSpectrum, but not stated specifically for projective varieties.

annotations.label: Regular functions on projective variety are constant

anchor.snippet: The only regular functions which may be defined properly on a projective variety are the constant functions

annotations.note: The result that global regular functions on a connected projective variety are constant is not formalized.

annotations.label: Parabola vs cubic at infinity

anchor.snippet: If we draw it, we get a parabola

annotations.note: This illustrative example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Bézout's theorem

anchor.snippet: Bézout's theorem on the number of intersection points between two varieties can be stated in its sharpest form only in projective space

annotations.note: Mathlib's Bezout files concern Bézout domains, not the intersection-number theorem for projective curves.

annotations.label: Real algebraic geometry

anchor.snippet: Real algebraic geometry is the study of real algebraic varieties

annotations.note: A field-of-study description, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Circle with no real points

anchor.snippet: is a circle if

annotations.note: This worked example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Semi-algebraic sets

anchor.snippet: semi-algebraic sets , which are the solutions of systems of polynomial inequalities

annotations.note: Semi-algebraic sets are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hyperbola branch as semi-algebraic set

anchor.snippet: the branch in the first quadrant is a semi-algebraic set defined by

annotations.note: This semi-algebraic example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Hilbert's sixteenth problem (ovals)

anchor.snippet: Decide which respective positions are possible for the ovals of a non-singular plane curve of degree 8

annotations.note: Hilbert's sixteenth problem is not formalized.

annotations.label: CAD computes topology of semi-algebraic sets

anchor.snippet: Cylindrical algebraic decomposition (CAD) allows the computation of the topology of semi-algebraic sets

annotations.note: Cylindrical algebraic decomposition is not implemented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Numerical algebraic geometry

anchor.snippet: called numerical algebraic geometry has been developed over the last several decades

annotations.note: A field-of-study description, not formalized.

annotations.label: Gröbner basis

anchor.snippet: A Gröbner basis is a system of generators of a polynomial ideal whose computation allows the deduction of many properties

annotations.note: Mathlib has monomial orders but no Gröbner basis definition (only doc references).

annotations.label: Emptiness criterion via Gröbner basis

anchor.snippet: V is empty (over an algebraically closed extension of the basis field) if and only if the Gröbner basis for any monomial ordering is reduced to {1}

annotations.note: No Gröbner basis machinery exists in Mathlib to state this criterion.

annotations.label: Doubly exponential degree of Gröbner bases

anchor.snippet: they may contain, in the worst case, polynomials whose degree is doubly exponential in the number of variables

annotations.note: A complexity bound on Gröbner bases, which Mathlib does not formalize.

annotations.label: Cylindrical algebraic decomposition

anchor.snippet: CAD is an algorithm which was introduced in 1973 by G. Collins to implement with an acceptable complexity

annotations.note: CAD is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tarski's quantifier elimination

anchor.snippet: Tarski's theorem asserts that, from such a formula, one may compute an equivalent formula without quantifiers

annotations.note: Mathlib has model theory and Presburger QE but not Tarski's quantifier elimination for real-closed fields.

annotations.label: CAD complexity

anchor.snippet: The complexity of CAD is doubly exponential in the number of variables

annotations.note: A complexity statement about CAD, not formalized.

annotations.label: Doubly exponential worst-case complexity

anchor.snippet: The basic general algorithms of computational geometry have a doubly exponential worst-case complexity

annotations.note: A complexity-theoretic claim, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Scheme

anchor.snippet: algebraic varieties were subsumed into Alexander Grothendieck 's concept of a scheme

annotations.note: AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme is the formalized notion of a scheme as a locally ringed space locally isomorphic to Spec of a ring.

annotations.label: Affine schemes antiequivalent to commutative rings

anchor.snippet: Their local objects are affine schemes or prime spectra which are locally ringed spaces which form a category which is antiequivalent to the category of commutative unital rings

annotations.note: AffineScheme.equivCommRingCat gives the equivalence AffineScheme ≌ CommRingᵒᵖ, i.e. the antiequivalence with commutative rings.

annotations.label: Delian problem

anchor.snippet: was to construct a length x so that the cube of side x contained the same volume as the rectangular box a 2 b

annotations.note: The Delian (cube-doubling) problem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Abelian varieties

anchor.snippet: the abelian varieties , which are the projective varieties whose points form an abelian group

annotations.note: Abelian varieties are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Analytic variety

anchor.snippet: An analytic variety over the field of real or complex numbers is defined locally as the set of common solutions of several equations involving analytic functions

annotations.note: Mathlib has analytic functions and manifolds but no notion of analytic variety.

annotations.label: Complex manifolds are complex analytic varieties

anchor.snippet: Any complex manifold is a complex analytic variety

annotations.note: Complex analytic varieties are not formalized, so this relationship is not stated.

annotations.label: Not all complex analytic varieties are manifolds

anchor.snippet: not all complex analytic varieties are manifolds

annotations.note: Complex analytic varieties are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: GAGA

anchor.snippet: as has been shown by Jean-Pierre Serre in his paper GAGA

annotations.note: Serre's GAGA theorems are not formalized in Mathlib.
